summary: Graham analyzes why 'list of N things' articles are so popular, arguing their compartmentalized structure makes them easier for both readers and writers by providing an exoskeleton that eliminates the need to follow a thread of reasoning. He compares them to cheeseburgers—safe, predictable, and hard to mess up—but warns that their greatest weakness is leaving almost no room for the genuine discovery that defines real essay writing.
key_points: Lists of N things are popular because their explicit, compartmentalized structure reduces cognitive load for readers
The format is 'fault tolerant'—readers can skip any point without losing the thread
Writers benefit too: the structure protects against dead ends and makes it easy to produce a valid draft quickly
The classic 5-paragraph essay taught in schools is just a disguised list of 3 things
The format's greatest weakness is that it leaves no room for genuine discovery—the defining characteristic of real essay writing
money_quotes: The list of n things is parallel and therefore fault tolerant.
The list of n things is in that respect the cheeseburger of essay forms. If you're eating at a restaurant you suspect is bad, your best bet is to order the cheeseburger.
The greatest weakness of the list of n things is that there's so little room for new thought. The main point of essay writing, when done right, is the new ideas you have while doing it.
Because you don't have room for new ideas, you don't have them.
If you don't know you're using this form, you don't know you need to escape it.
key_insight: The list of N things is the safest and easiest essay format for both writers and readers, but its compartmentalized structure prevents the open-ended discovery that makes real essay writing valuable.
